title: Technical Glossary: Big Data Analytics
text:
1. The Nature of Data

The 5 Vs of Big Data
The framework used to define the “Big” in Big Data: * Volume: The sheer scale of data (Terabytes to Zettabytes). * Velocity: The speed at which data is generated and processed (Real-time streams). * Variety: The different forms of data (Structured, Semi-structured, Unstructured). * Veracity: The quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness of the data. * Value: The ultimate goal—turning raw data into actionable insights.


Data Types (Structured vs. Unstructured)

Structured: Data that fits into predefined models (SQL tables, CSVs).
Semi-structured: Data with some organizational properties but no rigid schema (JSON, XML).
Unstructured: Data with no predefined model (Images, Videos, PDF text, Social Media posts).





2. Data Engineering & Management

ETL vs. ELT
The two primary methods for moving data from source to storage: * ETL (Extract, Transform, Load): Data is cleaned and transformed before being loaded into a data warehouse. Traditional and structured. * ELT (Extract, Load, Transform): Data is loaded into a data lake in its raw form and transformed later as needed. Modern and scalable.


Data Lakehouse
A modern architectural pattern (popularized by Databricks) that combines the low-cost storage of a Data Lake with the high-performance data management of a Data Warehouse. * Why it matters: It supports both BI (Business Intelligence) and AI (Machine Learning) on a single platform.
[Image of data lakehouse architecture]




3. Data Mining & Analytics

Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning

Supervised (Classification/Regression): The model is trained on labeled data (e.g., predicting if an email is “Spam” or “Not Spam”).
Unsupervised (Clustering/Association): The model finds hidden patterns in unlabeled data (e.g., grouping customers into “segments” based on behavior).



Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
An approach to analyzing datasets to summarize their main characteristics, often using visual methods. * Objective: To discover patterns, spot anomalies, and test hypotheses before applying complex mining models.




4. Visualisation & Storytelling

Visual Perception Principles (Gestalt Laws)
Principles that describe how the human eye perceives visual elements. * Proximity: Objects close to each other are perceived as a group. * Similarity: Objects that look alike are perceived as related. * Why it matters: Essential for designing intuitive dashboards that don’t overload the viewer’s “cognitive load.”


Data Storytelling
The practice of building a narrative around a set of data and its accompanying visualizations to help convey the “Why” behind the “What.” * Components: Data + Visuals + Narrative.




5. Methodological Rigour & Ethics

Bias & Fair Analytics
The systematic error introduced into sampling or testing by selecting or encouraging one outcome or answer over others. * Data Mining Risk: If historical data is biased (e.g., hiring data), the mining model will automate that bias.


Differential Privacy
A system for sharing information about a dataset by describing the patterns of groups within the dataset while withholding information about individuals.






AstucePro-Tip



In the professional world, a “Data Scientist” builds the models, but a “Big Data Architect” builds the pipelines that allow those models to run at scale. This glossary covers both perspectives.

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop
text: Ce module hybride, composé de deux jours de conférences et de trois jours de workshop intensif, constitue une étape charnière dans le parcours du Master DTDM. Il prépare les étudiants à la double exigence du stage professionnel et de la rédaction du mémoire de master, en les plongeant dans les questionnements propres à l’ère post-numérique. Des professionnels, des chercheurs et des anciens étudiants partagent leurs expériences, tandis que les workshops permettent de travailler concrètement sur la méthodologie et les livrables attendus.

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop

Il prépare les étudiants à la double exigence du stage professionnel et de la rédaction du mémoire de master, en les plongeant dans les questionnements propres à l’ère post-numérique. Des professionnels, des chercheurs et des anciens étudiants partagent leurs expériences, tandis que les workshops permettent de travailler concrètement sur la méthodologie et les livrables attendus.

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop
text:
Format pédagogique








Phase
Durée
Objectifs




Conférences
2 jours

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop
text:
Objectifs pédagogiques
À l’issue de ce module, les étudiants seront capables de :

Comprendre les enjeux contemporains de l’ère post-numérique et ses implications pour les organisations
Définir et formaliser un projet de stage cohérent avec leur parcours et leurs ambitions
Poser une problématique de recherche pertinente pour leur mémoire
Choisir et appliquer une méthodologie adaptée à leur sujet de mémoire
Gérer les aspects pratiques et administratifs du stage (convention, attendus, livrables)
Présenter et défendre une première version de leur problématique devant leurs pairs

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop
text:
Programme des Conférences (2 jours)

Jour 1 – Vivre et travailler dans l’ère post-numérique
Matin :

Conférence inaugurale : Qu’est-ce que l’ère post-numérique ? Ruptures, continuités et nouveaux paradigmes
Table ronde avec des professionnels de la transformation numérique : témoignages sur les évolutions des métiers
Questions/réponses avec le public étudiant

Après-midi :

Conférence : Intelligence artificielle générative et avenir du travail intellectuel
Présentation : Le stage en entreprise ou en laboratoire : enjeux, attentes et bonnes pratiques
Table ronde d’anciens étudiants du Master DTDM : retours d’expérience de stage et de mémoire



Jour 2 – Recherche, innovation et mémoire de master
Matin :

Conférence : La recherche en sciences sociales du numérique : méthodes et positionnements
Présentation : Les attentes du jury de mémoire : critères d’évaluation et d’excellence
Exemples de mémoires remarquables des promotions précédentes

Après-midi :

Conférence : Innovation et entrepreneuriat dans un monde post-numérique
Présentation des ressources universitaires : bibliothèques, bases de données, outils de gestion bibliographique
Clôture et transition vers le workshop

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop
text:
Programme du Workshop (3 jours)

Jour 3 – Définir son projet
Ateliers :

Exploration des thématiques de stage et de mémoire possibles
Exercice d’idéation : cartographie des centres d’intérêt et des compétences
Rédaction d’une première note d’intention (stage + mémoire)
Revue par les pairs et feedback des enseignants



Jour 4 – Méthodologie et structuration
Ateliers :

Formulation de la problématique et des hypothèses de recherche
Choix de la méthodologie : approche qualitative, quantitative ou mixte
Construction d’un plan de mémoire provisoire
Initiation aux outils de gestion bibliographique : Zotero, Mendeley
Coaching individualisé avec les enseignants référents



Jour 5 – Présentation et validation
Ateliers et soutenances blanches :

Présentation de la problématique et du plan de mémoire devant un jury d’enseignants
Feedback structuré et pistes d’amélioration
Validation des projets de stage (signature des conventions)
Clôture du module et conseils pour la suite

title: 2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop
text:

Intelligence artificielle générative et créativité

title: Foreign Language Course
text: Ce cours vise à renforcer les compétences linguistiques des étudiants dans une langue étrangère (principalement l’anglais) dans le contexte spécifique du numérique et de la transformation digitale. Il prépare les étudiants à évoluer dans des environnements professionnels et académiques internationaux.

title: Foreign Language Course
text: Ce cours vise à renforcer les compétences linguistiques des étudiants dans une langue étrangère (principalement l’anglais) dans le contexte spécifique du numérique et de la transformation digitale. title: Foreign Language Course
text:
Objectifs pédagogiques
À l’issue de ce cours, les étudiants seront capables de :

Comprendre et produire des textes professionnels en langue étrangère liés au numérique
Participer activement à des réunions, présentations et négociations en anglais
Rédiger des rapports, emails et documents techniques dans un registre professionnel adapté
Analyser des ressources académiques et industrielles en langue étrangère
Présenter un projet numérique ou une problématique de transformation digitale à l’oral

title: Foreign Language Course
text:
Contenu du cours

Module 1 – Vocabulaire et terminologie du numérique

Lexique de la transformation digitale, de l’IA et des technologies émergentes
Acronymes et jargon du secteur (SaaS, API, agile, MVP, KPI, etc.)
Exercices d’appropriation contextuelle du vocabulaire



Module 2 – Communication professionnelle écrite

Rédaction d’emails professionnels, de comptes-rendus et de rapports de projet
Structure et style des documents académiques et professionnels en langue étrangère
Révision et correction entre pairs (peer review)



Module 3 – Communication orale et présentations

Techniques de prise de parole en public en langue étrangère
Préparation et animation de réunions et de workshops
Pitchs et présentations de projets numériques
Simulation d’entretiens professionnels



Module 4 – Lecture et analyse de ressources spécialisées

Lecture critique d’articles académiques et de rapports industriels
Synthèse et restitution en français et en langue étrangère
Veille et curation de contenus numériques

title: Internship
text: Le stage constitue une immersion professionnelle ou de recherche fondamentale dans le cursus du Master DTDM. D’une durée de 2 à 6 mois, il permet à l’étudiant de mettre en pratique les compétences acquises au cours de la formation, de développer son réseau professionnel et de nourrir son mémoire de master par une expérience de terrain authentique.

title: Internship
text: Le stage constitue une immersion professionnelle ou de recherche fondamentale dans le cursus du Master DTDM. D’une durée de 2 à 6 mois, il permet à l’étudiant de mettre en pratique les compétences acquises au cours de la formation, de développer son réseau professionnel et de nourrir son mémoire de master par une expérience de terrain authentique.

title: Internship
text:
Objectifs
Le stage vise à :

Appliquer les connaissances théoriques et les méthodes acquises en Master dans un contexte professionnel réel
Développer des compétences opérationnelles spécifiques au secteur de la transformation numérique
Contribuer de manière significative à un projet ou à une mission au sein d’une organisation
Établir et développer un réseau professionnel dans le domaine du numérique
Alimenter la réflexion du mémoire de master à partir d’observations et d’expériences de terrain

title: Internship
text:

Critère

title: Internship
text:
Types d’organismes d’accueil
Les stages peuvent être effectués dans des structures variées :

Secteur privé

Entreprises numériques (startups, scale-ups, grands groupes tech)
Cabinets de conseil en transformation digitale (McKinsey Digital, Accenture, Capgemini, etc.)
Agences digitales et studios de design
Entreprises de toutes tailles ayant des projets de transformation numérique (industrie, finance, santé, retail, etc.)



Secteur public et institutionnel

Ministères et agences gouvernementales (DINUM, ANSSI, etc.)
Collectivités territoriales en cours de transformation numérique
Organisations internationales (ONU, OCDE, Commission européenne)



Recherche académique

Laboratoires de recherche en informatique, SIC, gestion ou économie numérique
Équipes universitaires françaises et étrangères
Instituts de recherche (INRIA, CNRS, etc.)
Thèses CIFRE en lien avec une entreprise partenaire

title: Internship
text:
Missions types
Les étudiants du Master DTDM peuvent se voir confier des missions telles que :

Diagnostic et audit de la maturité numérique d’une organisation
Pilotage d’un projet de transformation numérique ou d’innovation
Analyse de données et production de tableaux de bord
Conception d’une stratégie de contenu ou d’une expérience numérique
Veille technologique et réalisation de rapports prospectifs
Animation de la conduite du changement (communication, formation, accompagnement)
Développement d’une solution no-code ou low-code
Recherche et production d’un rapport académique sur une problématique numérique

title: Internship
text:
Encadrement et suivi

Tuteur professionnel
Le tuteur ou la tutrice au sein de l’organisme d’accueil est responsable de :

Définir les missions et les objectifs du stage
Accompagner l’étudiant au quotidien dans sa prise de poste
Évaluer la progression et les compétences développées
Compléter la fiche d’évaluation finale du stage



Enseignant référent
L’enseignant ou l’enseignante référent(e) à l’université assure :

Le suivi régulier de l’étudiant (au minimum deux points de suivi)
L’accompagnement dans l’articulation entre la mission de stage et le mémoire
La validation du rapport de stage

title: Internship
text:
Livrables attendus

Rapport de stage : document de 25 à 40 pages présentant l’organisme d’accueil, les missions réalisées, les compétences développées et une analyse critique de l’expérience.
Soutenance de stage : présentation orale de 15 minutes devant un jury (enseignant référent + éventuellement le tuteur professionnel), suivie de questions.
Fiche d’évaluation complétée par le tuteur professionnel.

title: Internship
text:
Conseils pour trouver un stage

Commencez tôt : la recherche de stage doit débuter au moins 3 à 6 mois avant la date de début souhaitée.
Mobilisez votre réseau : anciens étudiants du Master, LinkedIn, forums professionnels.
Ciblez vos candidatures : adaptez votre CV et votre lettre de motivation à chaque offre.
Exploitez les ressources universitaires : service des stages de l’université, offres diffusées par le département.
Pensez à l’international : un stage à l’étranger est un atout distinctif sur le marché de l’emploi.

title: Course Specification: Analysis, Management, Data Mining, and Visualisation of Big Data
text:
1. Module Overview
This module provides an intensive introduction to the lifecycle of Big Data. Students will transition from handling static, small-scale datasets to managing the velocity, volume, and variety of modern data environments. The course balances technical rigors (data engineering) with creative storytelling (data visualization).


2. Learning Outcomes
By the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. Architect data pipelines for acquisition, cleaning, and transformation. 2. Evaluate storage solutions for structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data (SQL vs. NoSQL). 3. Apply data mining techniques (Classification, Clustering, Regression) to extract patterns. 4. Synthesize complex findings into interactive dashboards and visual stories. 5. Critique data practices through the lens of methodological rigor and ethical responsibility.



3. The 12-Week Roadmap









Week
Phase
Topic
Key Tools (2026 Stack)




W1
Foundations
The 5 Vs of Big Data: Challenges & Opportunities
Apache Spark, Hadoop MapReduce


W2
Foundations
Data Acquisition & ETL Workflows (Cleaning & Prep)
Python (Pandas), dbt


W3
Management
Storage: SQL vs. NoSQL & Data Lakes
Snowflake, MongoDB, S3


W4
Management
Processing Large-Scale Data Environments
Databricks, PySpark


W5
Analysis
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) & Descriptive Stats
Jupyter Labs, Seaborn


W6
Mining
Supervised Learning: Classification & Regression
Scikit-Learn


W7
Mining

Scikit-Learn


W8
Mining

SHAP, LIME (Model XAI)


W9
Visualisation
Principles of Visual Perception & Design
Tableau / Power BI


W10
Visualisation

Streamlit / Plotly Dash


W11
Strategy
Visual Storytelling & Decision Support
Canva, Quarto (Interactive Reports)


W12
Capstone
Final Project Defense: Real-world Big Data Case
Full Stack






4. Assessment Methodology

4.1 Continuous Assessment (40%)

Bi-weekly Coding Labs (20%): Hands-on data cleaning and mining exercises.
Mid-term Data Audit (20%): Critiquing a provided “dirty” dataset for ethical and structural flaws.



4.2 Final Capstone Project (60%)
Students must select a realistic large-scale dataset (e.g., Global Weather, Logistics, or Financial Transactions) and perform a full “Raw-to-Insight” pipeline. - Technical Pipeline (20%): The ETL and Processing logic. - Mining Model (20%): The choice and accuracy of the mining technique. - Visual Story (20%): An interactive dashboard presenting the decision-support findings.




5. Ethical & Responsible Use
In 2026, Big Data is inseparable from AI Ethics. Throughout the module, we will address: * Bias Mitigation: Ensuring datasets do not reinforce historical inequities. * Privacy-Preserving Analytics: Introduction to Differential Privacy. * Rigorous Validation: Avoiding “data dredging” (p-hacking).






NotePrerequisites



Basic proficiency in Python and SQL is expected. Students unfamiliar with these should complete the “Data Prep Bootcamp” in the Welcome Kit.

title: Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Differentiate between Qualitative (Attitudinal) and Quantitative (Behavioral) user feedback. 2. Set up and deploy Unmoderated Remote Testing using Maze. 3. Analyze Heatmaps and Session Recordings to identify UI friction. 4. Calculate a System Usability Scale (SUS) score for their MVP.

title: Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration
text:
1.1 “What they say” vs. “What they do”
In a Master’s context, we prioritize behavioral data over opinions. * The “Lizard Brain” Effect: Users often say an app is “easy to use” even if they struggled for 5 minutes to find the “Submit” button. * The Think-Aloud Protocol: How to moderate a session where the user narrates their thoughts in real-time.

title: Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration
text:
1.2 Introduction to Behavioral Analytics
In 2026, AI-powered analytics (like Hotjar AI or Microsoft Clarity) automatically summarize user frustration. * Rage Clicks: When a user clicks a button 5 times in 2 seconds (indicates a broken link or slow response). * Dead Clicks: Clicking on non-interactive elements (indicates confusing UI design). * Drop-off Points: Identifying exactly where users exit the “Onboarding Funnel.”

title: Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration
text:
2.1 Setting up Maze Missions (45 mins)

Activity: Remote Testing Setup.
Task: Connect your Softr or FlutterFlow prototype to Maze. Create three “Missions” (e.g., “Sign up and list one item for sale”).
Logic: Define a “Success Path.” Maze will automatically track how many users deviate from it.

title: Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration
text:
2.2 Analyzing the “Evidence” (45 mins)

Activity: Session Replay Review.
Task: Install the Hotjar tracking snippet on your Softr app. Observe 3 “Ghost Sessions” (anonymous recordings).
Audit: Document every “Micro-Hesitation” (when the mouse hovers for >3 seconds without clicking).

title: Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration
text:
2.3 The SUS Score (30 mins)

Activity: Subjective Validation.
Task: Create a 10-question System Usability Scale survey.
Logic: Send it to 3 peers. Calculate the average score. A score below 68 indicates a “UX Emergency.”

title: Week 12: Final Demo Day & Technical Defense
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Pitch a functional digital solution to a technical and business audience. 2. Defend architectural choices (Stack selection, Data Schema, Logic). 3. Handle real-time technical Q&A regarding scalability and security. 4. Demonstrate a live, bug-free “Happy Path” user journey.

title: Week 12: Final Demo Day & Technical Defense
text:
1.1 The “High-Stakes” Format

5 Minutes: The Pitch. Focusing on the Problem, the Solution, and the Business Value.
5 Minutes: The Live Demo. A walkthrough of the functional app (Airtable/Softr/Make/AI).
5 Minutes: Technical Q&A. The Jury asks deep-dive questions about the “Back-end” logic.

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Explain the difference between a Database (Storage) and a Backend (Logic + Storage). 2. Understand the anatomy of a REST API Request (Method, Endpoint, Headers, Body). 3. Parse and structure JSON data objects. 4. Build and test a custom API Endpoint in Xano.

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text:
1.1 Why Xano? (Airtable vs. Xano)
While Airtable is an excellent “Internal Tool” database, Xano is a Turing-complete backend. - Scalability: Handling millions of records without performance lag. - Logic at the Source: Processing data before it reaches the frontend (e.g., calculating a discount server-side). - Security: Hiding complex business logic from the browser’s “Inspect Element.”

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text:
1.2 The API Conversation: “Hello, JSON”
An API is a conversation between two machines. - HTTP Methods: - GET (Give me data). - POST (Create new data). - PATCH (Update existing data). - DELETE (Remove data). - JSON: The “English” of the internet. - Example: {"status": "success", "data": {"id": 101, "name": "Sustainability Hub"}}

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text:
1.3 Authentication & Security

API Keys: The “ID Badge” for software.
Bearer Tokens: How we keep user data private in a No-Code environment.

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text:
2.1 Xano Environment Setup (30 mins)

Activity: Workspace creation and Data Import.
Task: Import your CSV or Airtable data into Xano. Observe the “Schema” automatically created.

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text:
2.2 Crafting the Endpoint (1 Hour)

Step 1: Create a GET endpoint named /get_my_items.
Step 2: Add a Query Parameter (e.g., category_id).
Step 3: Use the Function Stack to filter the database records based on that parameter.
Step 4: Run & Debug: Use Xano’s built-in debugger to see the “Raw JSON” output.

title: Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy
text:
2.3 Documentation & Testing (30 mins)

Activity: Exploring Swagger.
Task: Generate the automatic API documentation. Test the endpoint using an external tool (like Insomnia or Postman) to prove it works outside of Xano.

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Differentiate between Polling (Scheduled) and Webhooks (Instant) triggers. 2. Master Data Mapping between incompatible JSON structures. 3. Implement Conditional Logic using Routers and Filters. 4. Apply Error Handling strategies (Ignore, Resume, Rollback) to ensure system reliability.

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text:
1.1 Automation as Orchestration
In a modular “No-Code Stack,” Make acts as the conductor. * The Trigger: The event that starts the chain (e.g., “A new row in Airtable” or “An incoming Webhook from Softr”). * The Action: The operation performed (e.g., “Create a PDF,” “Send a Slack message”). * The Search: Looking up existing data to decide what to do next.

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text:
1.2 The Power of Webhooks
Instead of checking for updates every 15 minutes (Polling), we use Webhooks. * Instant Execution: The moment a button is clicked in Softr, the automation starts. * Data Payloads: Passing specific user data (ID, Email, Selection) directly into the workflow.

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text:
1.3 Computational Thinking: Routers & Filters

Filters: “Only proceed if Price > $100.”
Routers: Creating multiple paths.

Path A: If User = ‘Student’ -> Send Email.
Path B: If User = ‘Admin’ -> Post to Discord.

Iterators & Aggregators: How to handle lists of data (e.g., taking 10 individual line items and turning them into one single invoice).

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text:
2.1 The Instant Response Scenario (45 mins)

Activity: “The Notification Engine.”
Task: Create a Custom Webhook in Make. Connect a “Buy” button in Softr to this Webhook.
Mapping: Extract the User_Email and Item_Name from the Webhook payload.

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text:
2.2 Advanced Logic Branching (45 mins)

Activity: “The Smart Approval System.”
Task: Add a Router.

If the Item Category is “Electronics,” send it to a “Manual Review” table in Airtable.
If the Category is “Books,” automatically mark it as “Approved” and notify the user.

Logic: Use Filters on each route to check the Category value.

title: Week 7: Workflow Automation
text:
2.3 Debugging & Error Handling (30 mins)

Activity: “The Safety Net.”
Task: Intentionally break the scenario (e.g., use a wrong API key).
Learning: Inspect the “Bubbles” in Make to see exactly where the data failed. Implement a Break or Ignore directive to handle the error gracefully.

title: Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Apply Design Thinking principles to digital solution ideation. 2. Construct an Empathy Map to understand user friction. 3. Perform Rapid Ideation using the “Crazy 8s” methodology. 4. Define a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) based on feasibility and value.

title: Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design
text:
1.1 Design Thinking vs. Agile
In a Master’s context, we distinguish between doing the right thing and doing the thing right. - Design Thinking: The process of discovery. Empathizing with the user to ensure we are solving a real problem. - Agile Development: The process of delivery. Building in small, iterative increments to allow for pivoting. In No-Code, the distance between these two is almost zero.

title: Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design
text:
1.2 Empathy Mapping for Non-Technical Needs
Using the “Pain Point Log” from Week 1, students learn to map the user’s internal state: - Says/Does: What are the visible manual actions? - Thinks/Feels: What is the underlying frustration (e.g., fear of losing data, boredom)? - Pains/Gains: What is the cost of the current process vs. the dream state of automation?

title: Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design
text:
2.1 The “Crazy 8s” Challenge (45 mins)
Students take their primary pain point and fold a piece of paper into 8 sections. - Task: Sketch 8 different interface ideas or workflow steps in 8 minutes (1 minute per sketch). - Goal: Push past the first, obvious idea to find creative architectural solutions. - No-Code Twist: Instead of drawing code, students sketch User Flows (e.g., “Screen A -> Click Button -> Automation B -> Result C”).

title: Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design
text:
2.2 Defining the MVP Scope (45 mins)
Using the MoSCoW Method, students categorize the features they just sketched: - Must have: The core logic without which the app is useless. - Should have: Important but not vital for Version 1. - Could have: “Nice-to-have” features (often AI-related). - Won’t have (this time): Features that are too complex for a 12-week sprint.

title: Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design
text:
2.3 Peer-Co-creation Pitch (30 mins)
Students work in pairs. Each student has 3 minutes to present their MVP scope to a peer. The peer must act as a “Critical Friend,” asking: “How does this actually save the user time?”

title: Weekly Lab Assignments
text:
Phase 1: The Blueprint (Weeks 1-4)

Week 1: The Pain Point Log

Task: Observe your daily routines (personal or professional). Identify three specific manual processes that are repetitive, error-prone, or frustrating.
Deliverable: A simple document listing: 1) The Problem, 2) The current manual steps, 3) The desired outcome.



Week 2: Defining the Solution (Problem Statement)

Task: Select one problem from Week 1. Create a “User Persona” (who has this problem?) and a “Job-to-be-Done” (what are they trying to achieve?).
Deliverable: A 1-page PDF including a Problem Statement and a Basic User Journey Map.



Week 3: Architecting the Source of Truth (Airtable)

Task: Build the database for your project. You must have at least 3 Tables (e.g., Users, Items, Transactions) with at least 2 Linked Record fields.
Deliverable: A shared link to your Airtable Base and a screenshot of your Schema View.



Week 4: API & Backend Milestone (Xano)

Task: Create a Xano workspace. Import your Airtable data and build one custom API endpoint that filters data (e.g., “Show only items where Status = ‘Available’”).
Deliverable: Milestone 1 Report: A technical summary of your data structure and a link to your API documentation.





Phase 2: The Build (Weeks 5-8)

Week 5: Building the Web Portal (Softr)

Task: Connect Softr to your Airtable base. Set up a Signup/Login system. Create a “List Page” that shows data and a “Detail Page” for specific items.
Deliverable: A live URL to your Web App prototype.



Week 6: The Mobile Interface (FlutterFlow)

Task: Replicate your core feature in a mobile environment. Focus on State Management (e.g., clicking a button changes a variable or updates a record).
Deliverable: Access to your FlutterFlow project or a short video recording of the mobile navigation.



Week 7: Connecting the Nervous System (Make.com)

Task: Build an automation that connects at least three services.

Example: When a user submits a form (Softr) -> Add to Database (Airtable) -> Send a personalized email (Gmail).

Deliverable: A screenshot of your Make Scenario showing successful “green” execution history.



Week 8: Adding Intelligence (Stack AI)

Task: Integrate an AI agent into your workflow. The AI must perform a task like content categorization, sentiment analysis, or drafting a response.
Deliverable: A link to the “AI-augmented” feature in your app.





Phase 3: The Polish (Weeks 9-12)

Week 9: Peer-Review Audit

Task: Exchange project access with a classmate. Perform a “Stress Test.” Try to break their logic and document every bug or UI inconsistency you find.
Deliverable: A Peer-Review Report (2 pages) sent to your partner and the professor.



Week 10: User Testing Results

Task: Record a 5-minute session of a “real user” (friend, colleague) trying to use your app without instructions. Document their points of confusion.
Deliverable: A summary of 3 required UI/UX changes based on user feedback.



Week 11: Security & Final Technical Dossier

Task: Conduct a GDPR audit. Where is your data stored? How do you handle user deletion? Complete the final technical documentation.
Deliverable: Final Technical Dossier (PDF).



Week 12: Final Video Demo

Task: Record a high-quality 3-minute video demo of your functional MVP.
Deliverable: Final Project Submission (App URL + Video + Final Report).

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Differentiate between Deterministic Logic (Make.com) and Probabilistic Logic (AI Agents). 2. Implement RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) to connect AI to private data. 3. Master Prompt Engineering for structured data output (JSON). 4. Orchestrate multi-step AI workflows using Stack AI.

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
text:
1.1 From Chatbots to Agents
In 2026, we don’t just “chat” with AI; we build Agents. * The Chatbot: Answers questions based on general knowledge. * The Agent: Has a goal, access to tools (APIs), and can execute tasks autonomously. * The Architect’s Role: Defining the “Guardrails” and the “Tools” the agent can use.

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
section: 1.2 RAG: Giving the AI a Memory
text:
1.2 RAG: Giving the AI a Memory
Traditional LLMs have a cutoff date. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) allows the AI to “read” your specific Airtable or PDF files before answering. * Step 1: User asks a question. * Step 2: System searches your database for relevant “Chunks.” * Step 3: System sends the question + the chunks to the LLM. * Step 4: AI provides an answer based only on your data.

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
text:
1.3 Prompt Engineering for Developers
We treat prompts as “Functions.” * System Prompts: Defining the persona (e.g., “You are a Senior Sustainability Auditor”). * Output Formatting: Forcing the AI to respond in JSON so it can be parsed by Make.com or Softr.

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
text:
2.1 Stack AI Orchestration (45 mins)

Activity: Creating a “Content Categorization Agent.”
Task: Build a flow in Stack AI that takes a “User Input” and a “Knowledge Base” (your Airtable).
Logic: The AI must look at the user’s item description and categorize it into the correct campus department.

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
section: 2.2 Connecting AI to the Stack (45 mins)
text:
2.2 Connecting AI to the Stack (45 mins)

Activity: The API Bridge.
Task: Export your Stack AI flow as an API Endpoint.
Integration: In Make.com, add a module that sends new Airtable records to Stack AI and writes the “AI Summary” back into a specific Airtable field.

title: Week 8: AI-Native Development
text:
2.3 Testing Hallucinations (30 mins)

Activity: “Red Teaming.”
Task: Try to trick your agent into giving wrong advice or breaking character.
Fix: Refine the System Prompt to add “Negative Constraints” (e.g., “Do not mention prices outside of the $0-$500 range”).

title: Final MVP Project
text:
The final examination consists of designing, building, and presenting a functional digital solution (Minimum Viable Product) that addresses a specific “Personal or Organizational Need” identified during the course.


Every student must submit a “No-Code Solution Package” comprising: 1. The Functional App/Automation: A live URL or access to the digital environment. 2. The Technical Dossier: A 2,000-word report documenting the architecture. 3. The Demo Pitch: A 5-minute live presentation followed by 5 minutes of technical Q&A.



To demonstrate Master-level competency, the project must include: * A Relational Database: At least 3 linked tables with appropriate data types (Airtable or Xano). * Complex Logic: Use of conditional triggers (If/Then), filters, and at least one automated multi-step workflow (Make). * External Integration: At least one connection to an external API or AI service (e.g., OpenAI, Google Maps, Stripe). * User Management: A functional login/signup system with distinct user roles or permissions.



The report must follow this outline: * Executive Summary: The problem, the target user, and the chosen “Stack.” * System Architecture Diagram: A visual map of how data flows between your tools. * Data Schema: A detailed view of your database structure (fields, types, and relationships). * Scalability & Security Analysis: * How would this app handle 10,000 users? * Where is the data stored? Is it GDPR compliant? * What are the risks of “Vendor Lock-in” for this specific project? * Critical Reflection: What was the biggest technical hurdle, and how did you use AI or peer co-creation to solve it?











Criteria
Weight
Excellent (Grade A)




Problem-Solution Fit
20%
The app solves a clearly defined and validated pain point.


Architectural Integrity
30%
Database is normalized; workflows are efficient and error-handled.


UX/UI Design
20%
Professional, responsive, and intuitive interface.


Technical Complexity
20%
Effective use of APIs, AI agents, or complex logic branches.


Critical Awareness
10%
Clear understanding of security, ethics, and limitations.







Deadline: Week 12, 24 hours before the Demo Day.
Format: PDF for the report + Shared “Read-only” links for the No-Code tools (Airtable base, Make scenario, etc.).







AvertissementAcademic Integrity & AI



While the use of AI agents (like Replit Agent or GPT-4) is encouraged for generating logic or debugging, copy-pasting a full solution without understanding the underlying architecture will result in failure during the Oral Examination. You must be able to explain every node of your workflow.

title: Digital Foresight
text: La prospective numérique (Digital Foresight) est la discipline qui permet d’anticiper, d’analyser et de se préparer aux évolutions technologiques et sociétales à venir. Ce cours dote les étudiants des méthodes et des outils pour explorer les futurs possibles du numérique et élaborer des stratégies adaptées à long terme.

title: Digital Foresight
text: La prospective numérique (Digital Foresight) est la discipline qui permet d’anticiper, d’analyser et de se préparer aux évolutions technologiques et sociétales à venir. Ce cours dote les étudiants des méthodes et des outils pour explorer les futurs possibles du numérique et élaborer des stratégies adaptées à long terme.

title: Digital Foresight
text:
Objectifs pédagogiques
À l’issue de ce cours, les étudiants seront capables de :

Comprendre les fondements théoriques de la prospective et de la veille technologique
Identifier les signaux faibles et les tendances émergentes dans le domaine du numérique
Construire des scénarios prospectifs cohérents et argumentés
Utiliser des outils de veille et d’intelligence collective pour analyser l’environnement numérique
Intégrer la démarche prospective dans une stratégie d’organisation

title: Digital Foresight
text:
Contenu du cours

Module 1 – Fondements de la prospective

Histoire et courants de la prospective (Michel Godet, Hugues de Jouvenel)
Différence entre prévision, prédiction et prospective
La prospective stratégique appliquée aux organisations numériques
Introduction aux méthodes systémiques



Module 2 – Veille et identification des tendances

Méthodes de veille technologique et concurrentielle
Identification et qualification des signaux faibles
Outils numériques de veille : agrégateurs, outils d’IA, bases de données spécialisées
Analyse PESTEL appliquée au contexte numérique



Module 3 – Construction de scénarios

La méthode des scénarios (morphologie, axes stratégiques)
Atelier de construction de scénarios pour le numérique à horizon 2030–2040
Techniques d’animation de groupes prospectifs (Delphi, World Café)
Évaluation des scénarios : vraisemblance, impact, robustesse



Module 4 – Prospective et stratégie organisationnelle

De la prospective à la feuille de route stratégique (roadmap)
Gestion de l’incertitude et résilience organisationnelle
Cas d’études : entreprises ayant intégré la prospective numérique dans leur stratégie
Présentation des travaux prospectifs des équipes

title: Digital Foresight
text:
Technologies et outils abordés

Outils de veille : Feedly, Inoreader, Netvibes, Google Alerts
Cartographie : Kumu, MindMeister, Miro
Analyse des tendances : Gartner Hype Cycle, MIT Technology Review Emerging Technologies
IA et prospective : outils d’analyse sémantique et de traitement de données textuelles

title: Digital Foresight
text:
Méthodes pédagogiques







Format
Description




Cours magistraux
Apports conceptuels et méthodologiques


Ateliers prospectifs
Construction collective de scénarios


Études de cas
Analyse d’entreprises et de secteurs en mutation


Conférences invitées
Interventions de prospectivistes et de directeurs de l’innovation

title: Digital Foresight
text:
Évaluation

Rapport de veille (individuel) – 30 %
Scénarios prospectifs (travail de groupe) – 40 %
Présentation orale de la démarche prospective – 30 %

title: Digital Foresight
text:
Ressources recommandées

Michel Godet, Manuel de prospective stratégique (Dunod)
Gartner, Hype Cycle for Emerging Technologies (rapports annuels)
Institut pour la Prospective et la Sécurité en Europe (IPSE)
MIT Media Lab – Next Economy reports
OCDE, rapports sur l’avenir du travail et du numérique

title: Data and Transaction Security
text: Ce cours aborde les enjeux de sécurité dans les environnements numériques contemporains sous trois angles complémentaires : la cybersécurité pour la protection des données et des systèmes d’information, la blockchain comme technologie de registre distribué garantissant l’intégrité des transactions, et les NFT (Non-Fungible Tokens) comme nouvelle forme d’actif numérique. Les étudiants y acquièrent des connaissances théoriques et pratiques pour protéger et sécuriser les systèmes et transactions numériques.

title: Data and Transaction Security
text: Ce cours aborde les enjeux de sécurité dans les environnements numériques contemporains sous trois angles complémentaires : la cybersécurité pour la protection des données et des systèmes d’information, la blockchain comme technologie de registre distribué garantissant l’intégrité des transactions, et les NFT (Non-Fungible Tokens) comme nouvelle forme d’actif numérique. Les étudiants y acquièrent des connaissances théoriques et pratiques pour protéger et sécuriser les systèmes et transactions numériques.

title: Data and Transaction Security
text:
Objectifs pédagogiques
À l’issue de ce cours, les étudiants seront capables de :

Comprendre les principales menaces et vulnérabilités qui pèsent sur les systèmes d’information
Mettre en œuvre des bonnes pratiques de cybersécurité au niveau organisationnel
Expliquer les fondements techniques de la blockchain et ses applications
Distinguer les différents types de blockchain (publique, privée, de consortium)
Analyser les usages et les enjeux des NFT dans les économies numériques
Évaluer les risques juridiques et réglementaires associés aux transactions numériques

title: Data and Transaction Security
text:
Contenu du cours

Module 1 – Cybersécurité : fondamentaux et pratiques

Panorama des cybermenaces actuelles : phishing, ransomware, attaques DDoS, ingénierie sociale
Principes fondamentaux de la sécurité de l’information : confidentialité, intégrité, disponibilité (trilogie CIA)
Politiques de sécurité des systèmes d’information (PSSI)
Normes et référentiels : ISO 27001, NIST Cybersecurity Framework, recommandations ANSSI
Sécurité des applications web et des API
Gestion des incidents et plans de continuité d’activité (PCA / PRA)



Module 2 – Cryptographie et sécurité des transactions

Principes de la cryptographie symétrique et asymétrique
Signatures numériques et certificats SSL/TLS
Hachage cryptographique et intégrité des données
Protocoles sécurisés : HTTPS, SSH, PGP
Zero-knowledge proofs et privacy-preserving technologies



Module 3 – Blockchain : technologie et applications

Histoire et principes du Bitcoin et de la blockchain
Fonctionnement d’une blockchain : blocs, nœuds, consensus (Proof of Work, Proof of Stake)
Smart contracts et la plateforme Ethereum
Blockchains privées et de consortium : Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum
Applications sectorielles : finance, supply chain, santé, administration
Limites et défis de la blockchain (scalabilité, consommation énergétique)



Module 4 – NFT et économie numérique tokenisée

Qu’est-ce qu’un NFT ? Définition, standards techniques (ERC-721, ERC-1155)
Marchés et plateformes NFT : OpenSea, Rarible, Foundation
Usages des NFT : art numérique, gaming, identité numérique, tickets d’événements
Enjeux juridiques des NFT : propriété intellectuelle, droit d’auteur, régulation
DeFi (Finance Décentralisée) : principes et risques
Cadre réglementaire européen : règlement MiCA (Markets in Crypto-Assets)

title: Data and Transaction Security
text:
Méthodes pédagogiques







Format
Description




Cours magistraux
Apports théoriques et techniques


Travaux pratiques
Simulation d’audits de sécurité, déploiement d’un smart contract en environnement de test


Études de cas
Analyse de cyberattaques réelles, cas de fraudes liés aux NFT


Conférences invitées
Intervenants issus du monde de la cybersécurité et de la fintech

title: Data and Transaction Security
text:
Évaluation

Audit de sécurité (rapport écrit sur un système fictif ou réel) – 35 %
Projet blockchain (déploiement et documentation d’un smart contract en test) – 35 %
Note de synthèse sur un enjeu de régulation des crypto-actifs – 30 %

title: Data and Transaction Security
text:
Ressources recommandées

Cybersécurité :

ANSSI – Guides et bonnes pratiques
Bruce Schneier, Secrets and Lies (Wiley)
OWASP Top 10 (owasp.org)

Blockchain :

Satoshi Nakamoto, Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System (whitepaper original)
Primavera De Filippi & Aaron Wright, Blockchain and the Law (Harvard University Press)
Documentation officielle Ethereum (ethereum.org)

NFT et crypto-actifs :

Règlement MiCA (UE 2023/1114) – eur-lex.europa.eu
OpenSea Blog (tendances et enjeux du marché NFT)

title: Data and Transaction Security
text:
Prérequis
Des notions de base en informatique et en réseaux sont souhaitables, sans être obligatoires. Une appétence pour les sujets techniques est recommandée.

title: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management
text:
Bienvenue sur le site du Master DTDM de l’Université Paris 8.
Ce programme forme des professionnels capables de concevoir, piloter et gérer la transformation numérique des organisations, en articulant vision stratégique, compétences techniques et posture managériale.
crumbs: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management

title: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management
text:
Présentation du Master
Le Master Digital Transformation Design & Management (DTDM) est un programme de deuxième cycle universitaire qui prépare les étudiants à comprendre et à conduire la transformation numérique dans toutes ses dimensions : technologique, organisationnelle, juridique, éthique et stratégique.
Le programme allie cours magistraux, ateliers pratiques, conférences professionnelles et une immersion en entreprise afin de garantir une formation à la fois académique et opérationnelle.
crumbs: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management

title: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management
text:
Structure du programme
Le cursus est organisé en onze modules couvrant l’ensemble du spectre de la transformation numérique :

1. Introduction au No-Code
Introduction to No-Code Programming: Ideation around Personal Needs and Co-creation of Digital Solutions
Idéation autour des besoins personnels et co-création de solutions numériques sans code.


2. Langue Étrangère
Foreign Language Course
Renforcement des compétences linguistiques dans un contexte professionnel numérique international.


3. Digital Foresight
Digital Foresight
Méthodes et outils de prospective appliqués aux mutations numériques.


4. Digital Transformation Management
Digital Transformation Management (2 jours de cours + 3 jours de workshop)
Pilotage stratégique et opérationnel de la transformation numérique en organisation.


5. Droits Fondamentaux & IA
Fundamental Rights in the Era of Digital and AI
Enjeux juridiques et éthiques liés au numérique et à l’intelligence artificielle.


6. Sécurité des Données & Transactions
Data and Transaction Security (Cybersecurity, Blockchain, NFT)
Cybersécurité, blockchain et nouvelles formes de valeur numérique.


7. Big Data
Analysis, Management, Data Mining, and Visualisation of Big Data
Analyse, gestion, extraction et visualisation des mégadonnées.


8. Orientation Professionnelle
Professional Orientation (Research/Industry)
Accompagnement vers les métiers de la recherche ou de l’industrie numérique.


9. Conférences & Workshop
2 Days of Conferences & 3 Days Workshop: Internship and Master’s Thesis Preparation in the Post-Digital Era
Préparation au stage et au mémoire dans le contexte post-numérique.


10. Mémoire de Master
Master’s Thesis
Travail de recherche approfondi encadré par un directeur de mémoire.


11. Stage
Internship (2–6 months)
Immersion professionnelle en entreprise ou en laboratoire de recherche.
crumbs: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management

title: Master DTDM – Digital Transformation Design & Management
text:
Compétences visées

Concevoir et piloter des projets de transformation numérique
Maîtriser les outils no-code et les technologies émergentes
Analyser et valoriser les données massives
Comprendre les enjeux juridiques et éthiques du numérique
Anticiper les évolutions technologiques par la prospective
Communiquer en environnement professionnel international

title: Master’s Thesis
text: Le mémoire de master est le travail académique central du Master DTDM. Il constitue la preuve de la capacité de l’étudiant à mener une réflexion autonome, rigoureuse et originale sur un sujet en lien avec la transformation numérique. Encadré par un directeur ou une directrice de mémoire, ce travail est présenté et soutenu devant un jury à l’issue de l’année de master.

title: Master’s Thesis
text: Le mémoire de master est le travail académique central du Master DTDM. Il constitue la preuve de la capacité de l’étudiant à mener une réflexion autonome, rigoureuse et originale sur un sujet en lien avec la transformation numérique. Encadré par un directeur ou une directrice de mémoire, ce travail est présenté et soutenu devant un jury à l’issue de l’année de master.

title: Master’s Thesis
text:
Objectifs
Le mémoire de master vise à :

Démontrer la maîtrise des savoirs et des compétences acquis au cours du Master DTDM
Développer une démarche de recherche originale sur une problématique liée au numérique
Articuler des apports théoriques et une analyse empirique ou conceptuelle rigoureuse
Produire un document scientifique de qualité répondant aux standards académiques
Préparer les étudiants souhaitant poursuivre en doctorat ou exercer des fonctions requérant des compétences analytiques avancées

title: Master’s Thesis
text:
Caractéristiques du mémoire







Critère
Attendu




Volume
60 à 100 pages (hors annexes et bibliographie)


Langue
Français (ou anglais sur accord du directeur de mémoire)


Structure
Introduction · Revue de littérature · Méthodologie · Résultats · Discussion · Conclusion


Bibliographie
Minimum 40 références académiques (normes APA ou Chicago)


Soutenance
Présentation orale de 20 minutes + questions du jury

title: Master’s Thesis
text:
Thématiques possibles
Les sujets de mémoire doivent s’inscrire dans les axes thématiques du Master DTDM :

Stratégies et gouvernance de la transformation numérique
Intelligence artificielle, automatisation et mutations du travail
Droits fondamentaux, éthique et régulation du numérique
Usages numériques et comportements des utilisateurs

title: Master’s Thesis
text:
Encadrement et suivi
Chaque étudiant est suivi par un directeur ou une directrice de mémoire, enseignant-chercheur de l’Université Paris 8 ou professionnel habilité. L’encadrement comprend :

Des réunions régulières de suivi (minimum 4 séances dans l’année)
Des retours écrits sur les versions intermédiaires du mémoire
Un accès aux ressources documentaires de l’université (bibliothèque, bases de données)
Un accompagnement méthodologique (séminaires de méthodes, ateliers d’écriture)

title: Master’s Thesis
text:
Évaluation et jury
La soutenance se déroule devant un jury composé d’au moins deux membres :

Le directeur ou la directrice de mémoire
Un ou plusieurs membres extérieurs (enseignant-chercheur d’une autre université, professionnel du secteur)


Critères d’évaluation



Critère
Pondération

20 %

20 %

title: Master’s Thesis
text:
Conseils méthodologiques

Choisissez un sujet qui vous passionne : la rédaction d’un mémoire est un travail long et exigeant ; l’intérêt personnel est un moteur essentiel.
Posez une problématique claire et délimitée : mieux vaut traiter un sujet précis en profondeur qu’un sujet vaste en surface.
Constituez votre bibliographie dès le début et utilisez un logiciel de gestion (Zotero, Mendeley).
Écrivez régulièrement : n’attendez pas d’avoir tout lu pour commencer à rédiger.
Maintenez un dialogue régulier avec votre directeur : partagez vos avancées et vos doutes.
Respectez les échéances intermédiaires pour éviter la surcharge en fin d’année.

title: Grading Rubric
text:
Phase 1: The Blueprint (Weeks 1-4)

Week 1: The Pain Point Log

Task: Observe your daily routines (personal or professional). Identify three specific manual processes that are repetitive, error-prone, or frustrating.
Deliverable: A simple document listing: 1) The Problem, 2) The current manual steps, 3) The desired outcome.



Week 2: Defining the Solution (Problem Statement)

Task: Select one problem from Week 1. Create a “User Persona” (who has this problem?) and a “Job-to-be-Done” (what are they trying to achieve?).
Deliverable: A 1-page PDF including a Problem Statement and a Basic User Journey Map.



Week 3: Architecting the Source of Truth (Airtable)

Task: Build the database for your project. You must have at least 3 Tables (e.g., Users, Items, Transactions) with at least 2 Linked Record fields.
Deliverable: A shared link to your Airtable Base and a screenshot of your Schema View.



Week 4: API & Backend Milestone (Xano)

Task: Create a Xano workspace. Import your Airtable data and build one custom API endpoint that filters data (e.g., “Show only items where Status = ‘Available’”).
Deliverable: Milestone 1 Report: A technical summary of your data structure and a link to your API documentation.

title: Grading Rubric
text:
Phase 2: The Build (Weeks 5-8)

Week 5: Building the Web Portal (Softr)

Task: Connect Softr to your Airtable base. Set up a Signup/Login system. Create a “List Page” that shows data and a “Detail Page” for specific items.
Deliverable: A live URL to your Web App prototype.



Week 6: The Mobile Interface (FlutterFlow)

Task: Replicate your core feature in a mobile environment. Focus on State Management (e.g., clicking a button changes a variable or updates a record).
Deliverable: Access to your FlutterFlow project or a short video recording of the mobile navigation.



Week 7: Connecting the Nervous System (Make.com)

Task: Build an automation that connects at least three services.

Example: When a user submits a form (Softr) -> Add to Database (Airtable) -> Send a personalized email (Gmail).

Deliverable: A screenshot of your Make Scenario showing successful “green” execution history.



Week 8: Adding Intelligence (Stack AI)

Task: Integrate an AI agent into your workflow. The AI must perform a task like content categorization, sentiment analysis, or drafting a response.
Deliverable: A link to the “AI-augmented” feature in your app.

title: Grading Rubric
text:
Phase 3: The Polish (Weeks 9-12)

Week 9: Peer-Review Audit

Task: Exchange project access with a classmate. Perform a “Stress Test.” Try to break their logic and document every bug or UI inconsistency you find.
Deliverable: A Peer-Review Report (2 pages) sent to your partner and the professor.



Week 10: User Testing Results

Task: Record a 5-minute session of a “real user” (friend, colleague) trying to use your app without instructions. Document their points of confusion.
Deliverable: A summary of 3 required UI/UX changes based on user feedback.



Week 11: Security & Final Technical Dossier

Task: Conduct a GDPR audit. Where is your data stored? How do you handle user deletion? Complete the final technical documentation.
Deliverable: Final Technical Dossier (PDF).



Week 12: Final Video Demo

Task: Record a high-quality 3-minute video demo of your functional MVP.
Deliverable: Final Project Submission (App URL + Video + Final Report).

title: Practice Case Study: The Green Campus Exchange
text:
1. The Challenge: “The Green Campus Exchange”
Context: Your university has noticed a massive waste of resources (textbooks, electronics, and kitchen equipment) left behind by departing students. Meanwhile, incoming students struggle to afford new gear.
The Goal: You must build a functional “Circular Economy” MVP that allows students to list, borrow, or buy items within the campus community.



2. User Stories (The “Need”)
Your solution must satisfy three core user personas: 1. The Donor: Needs to quickly list an item (photo, category, condition) and choose if it’s “Free” or “For Sale.” 2. The Buyer/Borrower: Needs to browse items by category, filter by “Available” status, and request an item. 3. The Admin: Needs to validate listings before they go live to ensure they follow campus rules.



3. Technical Requirements
To pass this practice run, your architecture must include:

A. Data Architecture (Airtable)
Create a relational database with at least these tables: * Users: Names, emails, student IDs, and a “Rating” field. * Items: Linked to a User (Owner), Category, Status (Draft, Approved, Sold), and Price. * Transactions: Linked to an Item, a Buyer, and a Date.


B. The Interface (Softr)

A Member Area where students can see their own listings.
A Public Gallery with search and filter capabilities.
A Submission Form for new items.



C. Logic & Automation (Make.com)

Workflow 1: When a new item is submitted, send a notification to the Admin via email or Slack for approval.
Workflow 2 (AI Integration): Use an AI Agent (Stack AI or OpenAI) to automatically generate a “Marketing Description” for the item based on the student’s short title and category.





4. Success Criteria (The “Tech-Check”)
Before submitting your practice link, verify: - [ ] Can a user sign up and log in? - [ ] Does the “Add Item” form correctly populate the Airtable base? - [ ] Does the “Status” change to “Sold” automatically when a transaction is recorded? - [ ] Is the AI-generated description appearing in the item details?



5. Reflection Question (Short Answer)
In your documentation, answer the following: “If this platform grew from 100 students to 10,000, which part of your current No-Code stack would break first, and how would you fix it using a ‘Low-Code’ approach?”






AstucePro-Tip



Focus on the Data Integrity first. If your database is messy, your automations will fail!

title: Week 9: Co-Creation & Peer-Review Sprint
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Perform a Technical Audit on a third-party No-Code architecture. 2. Practice Pair-Building to solve complex logic bottlenecks. 3. Identify and document Edge Cases and “Logic Breaks.” 4. Implement Refactoring techniques to simplify automated workflows.

title: Week 9: Co-Creation & Peer-Review Sprint
text:
1.1 Hour 1: The Technical Swap & Audit

Activity: “The Handover.”
Task: Students swap access to their Airtable, Softr, and Make.com environments.
Audit Checklist: The “Auditor” must check:

Naming Conventions: Are fields in Airtable named clearly (e.g., fld_user_email)?
Logic Efficiency: Does a Make scenario use 10 modules when it could use 3?
Security: Are API keys exposed? Is conditional visibility actually blocking sensitive data?

title: Week 9: Co-Creation & Peer-Review Sprint
text:
1.2 Hour 2: The “Chaos Monkey” Session (Stress-Testing)

Activity: Trying to “Break” the app.
Task: The auditor attempts to input “bad data” into the partner’s app.

Example: Entering text in a number field, submitting an empty form, or clicking “Buy” ten times in a second.

Goal: Identify where the app crashes or where the database creates “Zombie Records” (empty or orphaned data).

title: Week 9: Co-Creation & Peer-Review Sprint
text:
1.3 Hour 3: Refactoring & Co-Creation

Activity: Collaborative Problem Solving.
Task: Based on the bugs found in Hour 2, the pair works together to fix the most critical logic flaw.
Logic: Practice explaining why a certain fix was implemented. This prepares students for the Final Technical Defense.

title: No-Code Technical Glossary
text:
Introduction
As a Master’s student, you must transition from being a “user” to being an architect. To achieve this, it is crucial to understand the “language” of digital systems, even if you are not writing traditional code. Here are the essential terms to get you started.



1. Data Foundations

Relational Database
Unlike a simple spreadsheet (like Excel), a relational database organizes information into separate tables that are linked together. * No-Code Example: In Airtable, a “Students” table is linked to a “Courses” table. * Why it matters: It prevents data redundancy and ensures data integrity across your entire system.


JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)
The universal standard format for exchanging data on the web. It is structured text using “Key/Value” pairs. * Example: {"LastName": "Smith", "FirstName": "John"}. * Why it matters: This is the format you will see flowing through your automations (Make, Zapier, Xano).




2. System Communication

API (Application Programming Interface)
An API is a “contract” that allows two applications to talk to each other. * Analogy: A waiter in a restaurant. You (the user) give an order (Request) to the waiter (API), who takes it to the kitchen (The remote system) and brings back your meal (The data). * Why it matters: This is what allows your app to fetch weather data, send a WhatsApp message, or connect to ChatGPT.


Webhook
A Webhook is a real-time notification sent from one application to another when a specific event occurs. * Analogy: It is an application “calling” yours to say, “Hey, something just happened!”. * Difference from API: An API waits to be asked (Pull), whereas a Webhook sends info as soon as it exists (Push).




3. Software Architecture

Frontend vs. Backend

Frontend: The visible part of your application (design, buttons, colors). Tools: Softr, FlutterFlow.
Backend: The invisible “brain” (database, logic, security). Tools: Xano, Airtable.
Headless: A “headless” architecture where the Backend and Frontend are entirely separate and communicate solely via API.



CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete)
The four basic operations that any application must be able to perform on data. 1. Create: Making a new profile. 2. Read: Viewing the profile. 3. Update: Changing a password. 4. Delete: Deleting the account.




4. Logic & Automation

Boolean Logic (If / Then / Else)
The foundation of all computing decisions. * Example: IF (If) stock is < 5, THEN (Then) send an email, ELSE (Else) do nothing.


Variables & Triggers

Trigger: The event that starts an automation (e.g., “New email received”).
Variable: A container for data that changes (e.g., the “Name” of the person who sent the email).








AstuceArchitect’s Tip



Do not try to memorize everything at once. During the Build Labs, refer back to this glossary whenever you encounter a technical term in the interface of Make or Airtable.

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Distinguish between a Flat Spreadsheet and a Relational Database. 2. Design a Database Schema using Primary and Foreign Keys (Linked Records). 3. Implement One-to-Many and Many-to-Many relationships. 4. Utilize Rollups, Lookups, and Formulas to create computed intelligence.

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text:
1.1 Why Spreadsheets Fail at Scale
In a Master’s context, we move away from “flat” data. - The Problem: Duplication. If one customer makes 10 orders, their address shouldn’t be typed 10 times. - The Solution: Normalization. Breaking data into its smallest logical parts (Tables) and connecting them.

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text:
1.2 The Anatomy of a Relationship

Primary Key: The unique identifier for a record (e.g., Student ID, Order Number).
Linked Records (Foreign Keys): How Table A “points” to Table B.
Relationship Types:

One-to-Many (1:N): One Teacher has many Students. (Most common).
Many-to-Many (N:M): Many Students attend many Classes. (Requires a “Junction Table”).

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text:
1.3 Computed Fields: The “Smart” Data

Lookup: Bringing a value from Table A into Table B via a link.
Rollup: Performing a calculation (Sum, Average, Count) on linked records.

Example: A “Project” table rolling up the total “Cost” from a linked “Tasks” table.

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text:
2.1 Schema Design (45 mins)

Activity: Map the database for the “Green Campus Exchange” (from the case study).
Task: Identify the 3 core tables: Users, Items, and Categories.
Logical Mapping: Sketch the connections. One Category has many Items. One User owns many Items.

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text:
2.2 Implementing in Airtable (45 mins)

Step 1: Create the tables and set the Primary Field (usually a Formula or ID).
Step 2: Use the “Link to another record” field type to connect them.
Step 3: Use a Rollup to count how many items each User has currently listed.

title: Week 3: Master Class in Data Modeling
text:
2.3 The Logic Test (30 mins)

Challenge: “If a user deletes their account, what happens to their items?”
Practice: Students must demonstrate how to filter a view to show only “Items from Users with a Rating > 4.”

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Differentiate between Responsive Web and Native Mobile UX patterns. 2. Master Layout Widgets (Rows, Columns, Stacks) in a Flutter-based environment. 3. Understand and implement App State vs. Page State. 4. Connect a mobile frontend to an external API (Xano/Airtable).

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
section: 1.1 Web vs. Mobile: Why it Matters
text:
1.1 Web vs. Mobile: Why it Matters
We don’t just “shrink” a website. Mobile design requires a shift in ergonomics: * Touch Targets: Minimum 44x44 pixels for thumbs. * Gestures: Using Swipes, Long-presses, and Pull-to-refresh. * Native Power: Accessing the hardware (Camera, GPS, Biometrics, Accelerometer).

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
section: 1.2 State Management: The “Memory” of the App
text:
1.2 State Management: The “Memory” of the App
In mobile apps, the app needs to “remember” things as you move between screens. * Local State: Data specific to one screen (e.g., text typed in a search bar). * App State: Data shared across the whole app (e.g., the User’s Cart or Login status). * Logic: Understanding when to trigger a “Rebuild” of the UI based on state changes.

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
text:
1.3 The Flutter Ecosystem
Students learn that FlutterFlow generates real Dart/Flutter code under the hood. * The “Low-Code” Escape Hatch: If a feature doesn’t exist, we can write a “Custom Action” in code. * Deployment: Understanding the difference between a PWA (Web) and the App Store/Play Store.

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
text:
2.1 The FlutterFlow UI Engine (45 mins)

Activity: Building the “Home Screen.”
Task: Use Columns and Rows to create a clean, centered layout.
Logic: Use Padding and Alignment to ensure the design looks good on both an iPhone 15 and a Google Pixel.

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
text:
2.2 Connecting Dynamic Data (45 mins)

Activity: The API Call.
Task: Use the API Manager to call the Xano endpoint created in Week 4.
Mapping: Map the JSON response to a ListView widget. Show students how to generate “Dynamic Children.”

title: Week 6: Native Mobile Development
text:
2.3 Navigation & Passing Parameters (30 mins)

Activity: The “Go to Details” Action.
Task: Create a second screen. Set up a “Navigate To” action when a list item is tapped.
Logic: Pass the ID of the item to the second screen so the app knows which data to fetch.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Apply Atomic Design principles to no-code web interfaces. 2. Connect a Frontend (Softr) to a Backend (Airtable/Xano) in real-time. 3. Implement Conditional Visibility based on user roles and permissions. 4. Design a seamless Login/Signup flow with protected pages.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text:
1.1 Atomic Design in No-Code
We don’t just “draw” pages; we build systems. We follow the Atomic Design methodology: * Atoms: Buttons, inputs, labels. * Molecules: A search bar (Input + Button). * Organisms: A navigation bar or a “List of Items” block. * Templates/Pages: The final assembly of these components.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text:
1.2 User Roles & Permissions
In a Master’s level app, not everyone sees everything. * Logged-out Users: Can see the Landing Page and “About” section. * Students: Can see “Available Items” and “My Profile.” * Admins: Can see “Pending Approvals” and “User Management.” * Logic: We define visibility at the Block level and Page level.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text:
1.3 UX Best Practices for Portals

Consistency: Using a “Global Style” guide (colors, typography).
Feedback Loops: Showing “Success” messages after a form is submitted.
Responsive Design: Ensuring the portal works on a laptop and a tablet.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text:
2.1 Connecting the “Face” to the “Brain” (30 mins)

Activity: Link Softr to your Week 3 Airtable base.
Task: Set up the API connection and map your “Users” table to the Softr User Management system.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text:
2.2 Building the “Browse & Detail” Flow (1 Hour)

Step 1: Add a Dynamic List Block. Map the fields (Title, Image, Price) to your Airtable columns.
Step 2: Configure the Detail Page. When a user clicks an item, Softr must open a dedicated page showing more info.
Step 3: Add a Search & Filter bar to allow users to find items by category.

title: Week 6: Web Interface Design & User Portals
text:
2.3 Implementing Permissions (30 mins)

Activity: Creating a “Seller’s Dashboard.”
Task: Add a block that is only visible to users whose “User Role” is “Seller.”
Logic: Test the app as two different users to ensure the visibility rules are functioning correctly.

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
text: By the end of this session, students will be able to: 1. Implement Privacy by Design principles (GDPR 2026 Compliance). 2. Identify and mitigate Security Vulnerabilities in No-Code stacks (API Leaks, Permissions). 3. Critically evaluate Vendor Lock-in and Data Sovereignty risks. 4. Conduct an Ethical Audit of AI-augmented workflows (Bias & Transparency).

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
text:
1.1 Beyond the “Playground” Phase
No-Code is no longer just for prototypes. In 2026, these apps handle real user data. * Data Sovereignty: Where is your data actually stored? (AWS US-East vs. EU-Central). * Vendor Lock-in: The “No-Code Trap.” What happens if your platform triples its price or goes bankrupt? * Strategy: Designing “Headless” architectures (separate DB and UI) to allow for easier migration.

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
text:
1.2 Security Pitfalls in No-Code

API Key Exposure: Never put a “Secret Key” in a Softr or FlutterFlow frontend. It can be found in 2 clicks via “Inspect Element.”
Airtable View Leakage: Sharing a “Base” link vs. a “Filtered View” link.
Orphaned Accounts: Ensuring that when a user is deleted in the Frontend, their sensitive data is also purged from the Backend (Xano/Airtable).

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
text:
1.3 The Ethics of AI Agents

Algorithmic Bias: Does your “Green Campus” AI favor certain categories or languages?
Transparency: Does the user know they are interacting with an AI agent?
Environmental Impact: Understanding the “Carbon Cost” of running 1,000 AI automations daily.

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
text:
2.1 The Security Scan (45 mins)

Activity: “Penetration Testing” your own app.
Task: Check every API Call in your Make.com and Xano logs. Is any sensitive info (Passwords, IDs) being sent in “Plain Text”?
Fix: Implement Environment Variables for all keys. Ensure all Softr blocks have “Logged-in User” visibility enabled.

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
section: 2.2 The GDPR “Right to be Forgotten” (45 mins)
text:
2.2 The GDPR “Right to be Forgotten” (45 mins)

Activity: Automating Compliance.
Task: Build a Make.com Scenario triggered by a “Delete Account” button.
Logic: The scenario must:

Delete the User from Softr.
Delete the User’s records from Airtable.
Send a confirmation email. This is the difference between a student project and a professional product.

title: Week 11: Security, Ethics & Governance
text:
2.3 Governance & Documentation (30 mins)

Activity: “The Handover Document.”
Task: Fill out the Security Section of your Final Technical Dossier.
Logic: List every tool in your stack and their respective data processing locations.

Visual Programming Weekly: Reviews new features in FlutterFlow and Xano.
Makerpad / Zapier University: Excellent for fundamental automation concepts.



2. Professional Communities

100 Days of No-Code: Great for daily challenges and peer support.
NoCodeDev Slack: A technical community for those moving from No-Code to Low-Code.





📝 Academic & Strategic Reading

Essential Papers & Articles (Digital Transformation)

The Rise of the Citizen Developer (2026 Gartner Report): Analysis of how 80% of technology products are now built by those outside traditional IT roles.
“Architecture without Code” (MIT Sloan Management Review): A strategic look at the decoupling of data, logic, and interface in modern enterprise.
Ethics in Autonomous No-Code Systems: A 2025 paper on the responsibility of architects when deploying AI-driven automations.



Books for Your Shelf

The No-Code Manifesto by various contributors (Focuses on the democratisation of creation).
Atomic Design by Brad Frost (The fundamental philosophy behind Softr and FlutterFlow UI).
Thinking in Systems by Donella Meadows (Essential for understanding how variables and triggers interact).





🚀 2026 Trend Watch: What’s Next?

The “Agentic” Shift: > In 2026, we have moved from simple automation (If This Then That) to Agentic Orchestration. Your role as an architect is no longer just linking apps, but managing the “permissions” and “memory” of AI agents.


Key Terms to Research:

Vector Databases: How AI “remembers” your project data (Pinecone, Weaviate).
Semantic Interoperability: When different No-Code tools “understand” each other without manual mapping.
Edge Computing in No-Code: Running logic locally on the user’s phone for privacy and speed.








AstuceHow to stay relevant



The No-Code ecosystem changes every 48 hours. Don’t fall in love with a tool; fall in love with the logic. If a tool disappears tomorrow, your ability to model data and map workflows remains your greatest asset.

title: Introduction to No-Code Programming
text: This course explores the transformative power of No-Code and Low-Code paradigms in the modern digital economy. It bridges the gap between human-centered ideation and functional digital creation. Students will move from identifying personal and organizational “pain points” to architecting, building, and co-creating sophisticated digital solutions (apps, automations, and workflows) without traditional syntax-based programming.

title: Introduction to No-Code Programming
text:
4.1 Phase 1: Ideation & Data Architecture

4.1.1 Week 1: The No-Code Paradigm & 2026 Ecosystem

Objectives: Understand the shift from syntax-based to logic-based development.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): History of No-Code; the “Citizen Developer” in 2026.
Lab (2h): Setting up the “Professional Stack.” Exploring the interfaces of Airtable, Softr, and Make.

Independent Study: Keep a “Pain Point Log” (Identify 3 real-world frustrations).
course materials



4.1.2 Week 2: Ideation & Human-Centered Design

Objectives: Transform personal needs into digital problem statements.
Contact Hours (3h):

Seminar (1h): Design Thinking vs. Agile. Empathy mapping for non-technical users.
Workshop (2h): “Crazy 8s” sketching. Defining the MVP (Minimum Viable Product) scope.

Independent Study: Finalize your Project Proposal (Problem/Solution fit).
course materials



4.1.3 Week 3: Master Class: Data Modeling (Airtable)

Objectives: Build a relational database from scratch.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): Relational logic: One-to-Many vs. Many-to-Many.
Lab (2h): Building your project’s “Source of Truth.” Linking records and creating rollups.

Independent Study: Populate your database with “Dummy Data” for testing.
course materials



4.1.4 Week 4: Advanced Backend & The API Economy (Xano)

Objectives: Learn server-side logic and external data communication.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): Understanding REST APIs, JSON, and Webhooks.
Lab (2h): Building an API endpoint in Xano to fetch and filter data.

Independent Study: Milestone 1 Submission: Technical Schema and API Map.
course materials

title: Introduction to No-Code Programming
text:
4.2 Phase 2: Interface & Logic Integration

4.2.1 Week 5: Web Interface Design & User Portals (Softr)

Objectives: Create responsive web interfaces connected to dynamic data.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): UI/UX Best Practices for No-Code Portals.
Lab (2h): Setting up conditional visibility and user permissions.

Independent Study: Map out your app’s navigation flow.
course materials



4.2.2 Week 6: Native Mobile Development (FlutterFlow)

Objectives: Explore native mobile functionalities (GPS, Camera, Push).
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): Mobile UX constraints vs. Web Desktop.
Lab (2h): Drag-and-drop UI build in FlutterFlow. Connecting a data source.

Independent Study: Optimize one key mobile feature for your MVP.
course materials



4.2.3 Week 7: Workflow Automation (Make.com)

Objectives: Build the “Invisible Nervous System” of your application.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): Automation logic: Triggers, Routers, and Error Handling.
Lab (2h): Building a multi-step automation (e.g., Form -> DB -> AI -> Email).

Independent Study: Stress-test your automation with various data inputs.
course materials



4.2.4 Week 8: AI-Native Apps & Agentic Workflows

Objectives: Integrate Large Language Models (LLMs) into No-Code apps.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): Prompt Engineering and API orchestration with AI.
Lab (2h): Building an AI agent using Stack AI to automate content generation.

Independent Study: Integrate an AI feature into your final project.
course materials

title: Introduction to No-Code Programming
text:
4.3 Phase 3: Co-Creation & Final Delivery

4.3.1 Week 9: Co-Creation & Peer-Review Sprint

Objectives: Practice collaborative development and technical auditing.
Contact Hours (3h):

Interactive Workshop (3h): “Pair-Building.” Students swap projects to debug and optimize each other’s logic.

Independent Study: Implement improvements based on peer feedback.
course materials



4.3.2 Week 10: User Testing & Analytical Iteration

Objectives: Use data to refine the User Experience.
Contact Hours (3h):

Seminar (1h): Qualitative vs. Quantitative feedback. Using Maze and Hotjar.
Lab (2h): Running a live user test and documenting “friction points.”

Independent Study: Final UI/UX polish of the MVP.
course materials



4.3.3 Week 11: Governance, Security & Ethics

Objectives: Deploy solutions that are safe, legal, and professional.
Contact Hours (3h):

Lecture (1h): GDPR compliance, data encryption, and vendor lock-in risks.
Workshop (2h): Conducting a Security Audit of your MVP.

Independent Study: Complete the Final Technical Dossier.
course materials



4.3.4 Week 12: Final Demo Day & Technical Defense

Objectives: Present and defend the technical architecture of your solution.
Contact Hours (3h):

Event (3h): 5-minute Pitch + 5-minute Live Demo + Q&A with Faculty.

Independent Study: Post-course reflection: “The Future of My Digital Solution.”
course materials

title: Fundamental Rights in the Era of Digital and AI
text: Ce cours explore les tensions et les articulations entre les droits fondamentaux reconnus par les démocraties libérales et le développement accéléré des technologies numériques et de l’intelligence artificielle. Il offre aux étudiants un cadre juridique, éthique et philosophique pour aborder les enjeux contemporains liés à la vie privée, à la non-discrimination, à la liberté d’expression et à la dignité humaine dans un monde de plus en plus numérisé.
